provenance.source_corpus: Ecohydrological gradients and their restoration on the periphery of extracted peatlands

source_spans.text: The moss layer transfer technique is effective at restoring extracted peatland surfaces.
The moss layer transfer technique is effective at restoring extracted peatland surfaces.
We then clear-cut peripheral strips, completely filled perimeter canals, and smoothed peripheral slopes around sections of the extracted surfaces to assess whether hydrological conditions improved.
We then clear-cut peripheral strips, completely filled perimeter canals, and smoothed peripheral slopes around sections of the extracted surfaces to assess whether hydrological conditions improved.
We then clear-cut peripheral strips, completely filled perimeter canals, and smoothed peripheral slopes around sections of the extracted surfaces to assess whether hydrological conditions improved.
We then clear-cut peripheral strips, completely filled perimeter canals, and smoothed peripheral slopes around sections of the extracted surfaces to assess whether hydrological conditions improved.
After these management actions, water tables increased to within approximately 5 cm of those along natural ecotones.
The elevation gradient is steep, and water tables drop steeply within 8 m of blocked perimeter canals, with possible effects at 25 m.
Lowering the peatland water table by 5 to 25 cm can increase the soil temperature and stimulate decomposition
Lowering the peatland water table by 5 to 25 cm can increase the soil temperature and stimulate decomposition
Lowering the peatland water table by 5 to 25 cm can increase the soil temperature and stimulate decomposition
and also encourage the growth of vascular plants, especially pioneer herbs, ericaceous shrubs and trees
while reducing the cover of Sphagnum and other typical peatland mosses
The increase in taller woody plants also produces a positive feedback on the water table
by increasing losses from interception of precipitation and evapotranspiration
by increasing losses from interception of precipitation and evapotranspiration
The drainage of peatlands reduces surface moisture conditions
The drainage of peatlands reduces surface moisture conditions
the water table rise may facilitate the establishment of Sphagnum species
